real_meaning: ～てしまう expresses two closely related feelings: (1) that an action was completed fully and irreversibly — it's done and there's no going back — or (2) that something happened that the speaker regrets, didn't intend, or finds unfortunate. The key emotional undercurrent is a sense of finality with an edge of 'uh oh' or 'well, that's that.' Even when there's no regret, the speaker is signaling they see the completion as notable or final.

formation.rule: Attach しまう to the て-form of a verb. て-form + しまう. For example: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてしまう, <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>む → <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでしまう (note: て-form of ぬ/む/ぶ verbs uses で, so it becomes でしまう), <ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れる → <ruby>忘<rt>わす</rt></ruby>れてしまう. In casual speech it contracts to ～ちゃう (from てしまう) or ～じゃう (from でしまう): <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べちゃう, <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んじゃう.
formation.common_mistake: Attaching しまう to the dictionary form instead of the て-form. Wrong: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるしまう. Correct: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてしまう.

sentences.jp: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>、もうやっちゃった。
sentences.notes: Uses the contracted ちゃった form; here the nuance is pure completion — the speaker is done and it's final, possibly with a hint of satisfaction or surprise at themselves.

sentences.jp: <ruby>大事<rt>だいじ</rt></ruby>なメールを<ruby>消<rt>け</rt></ruby>してしまいました。
sentences.translation: I accidentally deleted an important email.
sentences.notes: The classic regret use — しまう signals the speaker did something they wish they hadn't; the action is irreversible and they feel bad about it.

sentences.jp: <ruby>報告書<rt>ほうこくしょ</rt></ruby>はすでに<ruby>提出<rt>ていしゅつ</rt></ruby>してしまっておりますので、<ruby>変更<rt>へんこう</rt></ruby>はできかねます。
sentences.translation: Since the report has already been submitted, changes cannot be made.
sentences.notes: In formal/written contexts, してしまう emphasizes irreversible completion rather than personal regret — it's a polite way of saying 'it's already done and that can't be undone.'

sentences.jp: この<ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>、<ruby>泣<rt>な</rt></ruby>けてしまうんだよね。
sentences.translation: This movie just makes me cry, you know.
sentences.notes: A surprising use: the speaker isn't really lamenting — they're describing an involuntary emotional reaction. しまう here captures 'it happens despite myself,' which shows the pattern isn't always about regret but about something beyond one's control.

confused_with.contrast: Both involve completing an action, but the feeling is almost opposite. ～てしまう says 'it ended up done (often regrettably, or irreversibly),' while ～ておく says 'I'm doing this now intentionally as preparation for later.' Compare: <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやってしまった means 'I went and finished my homework (it's done, perhaps with relief or mild regret),' whereas <ruby>宿題<rt>しゅくだい</rt></ruby>をやっておいた means 'I did my homework ahead of time (deliberately, so I'm prepared).' Use しまう when the focus is on finality or an unintended outcome; use おく when you're acting with future purpose in mind.

bunpro_tip: Picture a door slamming shut — that's てしまう. The action is complete, the door is closed, and you may or may not be happy about it. When you drill it on BunPro, ask yourself: 'Is this a door slamming with relief, regret, or inevitability?' All three fit; what unites them is that the door is shut for good.
